text: This patient presented to my clinic with a near obstructing mass of the sigmoid colon. Biopsy showed adenocarcinoma. CEA was measured at fourteen point two. CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis did not show any evidence of metastatic disease. During colonoscopy, a tattoo was placed adjacent to the mass.

Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon encounters adhesions in the right lower quadrant due to the desmoplastic reaction of the colon cancer. The separation of these planes results in moderate bleeding, and a peritoneal margin is taken, which is visually confirmed to be negative for local cancer spread.

The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection of the mesocolon, incising its base to separate it from the retroperitoneal space. A broad window is created in the mesocolon, ensuring the ureter and gonadal vessels remain in the retroperitoneal space. Vermiculation confirms the ureter's identity, located medial to the gonadal vessels. The vessel sealer is used to dissect anterior to the ureter towards the rectosigmoid junction. The inferior mesenteric artery is transected at the base with a vascular stapler, while superior hemorrhoidal arteries remain with the specimen. The proximal transection point is identified, and the mesentery is transected towards the colon.

The surgeon observes the proximal margin of perfusion in the redundant sigmoid colon after injecting ICG, scoring the colon with cautery at this site for future reference. The sigmoid colon is retracted from the pelvis to reveal the distal margin of perfusion, which is visually confirmed to be more than sufficient. The distal margin is also scored to maintain visibility as the ICG diffuses over time.

The surgeon prepares the specimen for distal and proximal transection, performing an intracorporeal anastomosis. A 29mm EEA anvil is introduced into the abdomen, and a proline suture tied to the anvil is used to create a purse string for suturing the anvil into the proximal colon. After transecting the specimen, the sigmoid colon is removed from the pelvis and set aside. The proximal staple line is excised using scissors and electric cautery. The anvil is placed into the proximal colon, and the posterior enclosure is completed by holding the mucosal edges at the base of the anvil with a 2-0 proline suture for a secure anastomosis.

The distal portion of the EEA stapler is inserted through the rectum, and the stapler components are joined to create an end-to-end anastomosis. A second dose of ICG is injected to verify adequate perfusion of the anastomosis. Robotic suturing is used to oversow the anterior anastomosis for reinforcement. The surgeon performs a leak test by occluding the proximal colon and insufflating air distally, observing no air bubbles for a negative leak test.

This patient was discharged home on post-operative day two. The pathology report identified a nine point five centimeter colon cancer. All margins were negative. Twenty seven lymph nodes were collected, all of which were negative for metastatic disease. Thank you for watching.
